question: What is digestion?
answer: Breaking large insoluble food molecules into small soluble ones
accept: breaking big food molecules into smaller ones that can be absorbed
misconceptions: chewing food up in the mouth only
getting rid of waste from the body

question: In order, name the five main parts food passes through from the mouth onwards.
answer: mouth
oesophagus
stomach
small intestine
large intestine
accept: gullet for oesophagus
misconceptions: food goes to the liver as it passes through
large intestine comes before the small intestine

question: Why do we need to digest the carbohydrates in a potato before our bodies can use them?
answer: the carbohydrates are large and insoluble
so they cannot diffuse into the blood until broken into small soluble molecules
accept: insoluble molecules cannot pass into the bloodstream
misconceptions: the potato is poisonous until cooked
digestion makes the food bigger

question: Describe two things that happen to food in the mouth.
answer: it is chewed and broken down by the teeth
saliva (containing enzymes) mixes with it / moistens it to make swallowing easier
accept: enzymes in saliva break down carbohydrates
food is moistened so it is easier to swallow
misconceptions: acid is added in the mouth
most digestion is completed in the mouth

question: What is an enzyme?
answer: a protein that speeds up the breakdown of food
accept: a protein that speeds up chemical reactions such as digestion
misconceptions: a type of acid
a kind of food molecule
a sugar

question: In which four parts of the body are enzymes released to break down food?
answer: mouth, stomach, pancreas and small intestine
options: mouth, stomach, pancreas and small intestine
mouth, oesophagus, liver and large intestine
stomach, liver, large intestine and rectum
oesophagus, stomach, small intestine and anus
distractors: the oesophagus only pushes food and the liver makes bile, neither releases digestive enzymes
the liver makes bile not enzymes, and the large intestine absorbs water rather than releasing enzymes
the oesophagus only transports food and the anus removes waste, so neither releases enzymes
misconceptions: the liver releases digestive enzymes
the large intestine releases enzymes to break down food

question: Why are different enzymes released in different parts of the gut?
answer: different enzymes break down different nutrients
so several types are needed to digest all the food substances
accept: each enzyme works on a particular type of food molecule
misconceptions: one enzyme can break down every type of food
enzymes are only needed in the stomach

question: A person swallows food while lying upside down. Why does the food still reach the stomach?
answer: rings of muscle in the oesophagus contract to push the food down
options: rings of muscle in the oesophagus contract to push the food down
gravity pulls the food down to the stomach
the stomach sucks the food up the oesophagus
the food dissolves and flows to the stomach
distractors: food does not fall because of gravity, which is why it still works upside down
the stomach does not pull food up; the oesophagus pushes it along by muscle action
food is not yet dissolved in the oesophagus, and it is muscles that move it
misconceptions: food falls to the stomach because of gravity
the stomach pulls food down

question: Give two reasons why the stomach contains hydrochloric acid.
answer: it helps the enzymes work better
it kills bacteria in the food
accept: it creates the right conditions for enzymes
it destroys microbes in the food
misconceptions: the acid absorbs nutrients
the acid pushes food to the small intestine

question: A pupil says the liver is not part of digestion because food does not pass through it. Why is this wrong?
answer: the liver makes bile, which mixes with food to help digestion
options: the liver makes bile, which mixes with food to help digestion
food does actually pass straight through the liver
the liver releases hydrochloric acid into the stomach
the liver absorbs nutrients into the blood
distractors: food does not pass through the liver, but it still contributes bile
hydrochloric acid is made in the stomach, not the liver
nutrients are absorbed in the small intestine, not the liver
misconceptions: the liver is not part of digestion because food does not pass through it
the liver makes hydrochloric acid

question: What is bile and where is it made?
answer: a greenish liquid made in the liver that helps digest food
accept: a green fluid from the liver that aids digestion
misconceptions: bile is made in the stomach
bile is an enzyme

question: Where are most of the nutrients from digested food absorbed into the bloodstream?
answer: the small intestine
options: the small intestine
the stomach
the large intestine
the oesophagus
distractors: the stomach churns and chemically digests food but does not absorb most nutrients
the large intestine absorbs water, not the bulk of the nutrients
the oesophagus only pushes food towards the stomach
misconceptions: most nutrients are absorbed in the stomach
most digestion and absorption happens in the stomach

question: A pupil thinks most digestion happens in the stomach. Explain why this is a misconception.
answer: digestion happens all along the gut, not just in the stomach
the small intestine does much of the breakdown of food
and the small intestine also absorbs most of the nutrients
accept: enzymes act in the mouth, stomach, pancreas and small intestine
the small intestine does most of the absorption
misconceptions: all digestion is finished in the stomach
the stomach absorbs the nutrients

question: How do villi help the small intestine do its job?
answer: they increase the surface area of the small intestine
so more food molecules can be absorbed by diffusion
accept: larger surface area means faster absorption of nutrients
misconceptions: villi release enzymes
villi push food along the gut

question: Give three ways the small intestine is adapted to absorb food molecules efficiently.
answer: villi increase the surface area for absorption
microvilli increase the surface area even more
the walls are only one cell thick so molecules pass through quickly / it has a large network of blood vessels to carry molecules away
accept: thin (one-cell-thick) walls
good blood supply to carry away absorbed molecules
misconceptions: it has a thick wall to protect itself
it is short to speed food up

question: By what process do food molecules pass from the small intestine into the bloodstream?
answer: diffusion
accept: they diffuse across the wall
misconceptions: digestion
churning

question: What two things happen in the large intestine?
answer: water is absorbed into the bloodstream
leaving behind a more solid mass of waste (faeces)
accept: water is taken back into the body
solid waste / faeces is left behind
misconceptions: most nutrients are absorbed here
enzymes break down most of the food here

question: What is the solid waste called that leaves the body through the anus?
answer: faeces
accept: faeces / stools
misconceptions: urine
bile

question: In a surface area demonstration, a beaker started with 1000 cm3 of coloured water and 350 cm3 was absorbed by the towel. How much water was left in the beaker?
working: water left = starting volume - volume absorbed = 1000 - 350 = 650 cm3
misconceptions: adding instead of subtracting to give 1350
forgetting the units

question: A chocolate bar is broken into 4 equal chunks. Each chunk has a surface area of 6 cm2. What is the total surface area of all four chunks?
answer: 24 cm2
working: total surface area = number of chunks x surface area of each = 4 x 6 = 24 cm2
misconceptions: leaving the answer as 6 for one chunk
forgetting to multiply by the number of chunks

question: Where do most gut bacteria live?
answer: the large intestine
options: the large intestine
the stomach
the mouth
the oesophagus
distractors: the stomach's hydrochloric acid kills most bacteria rather than housing them
most gut bacteria are not found in the mouth, even though some bacteria live there
the oesophagus only transports food and is not where gut bacteria mainly live
misconceptions: most gut bacteria live in the stomach
gut bacteria mainly live in the small intestine

question: Which statement about gut bacteria is correct?
answer: many gut bacteria are helpful, not harmful
options: many gut bacteria are helpful, not harmful
all bacteria are harmful and cause disease
gut bacteria only ever make us ill
bacteria can never live inside a healthy body
distractors: not all bacteria are harmful; many gut bacteria help us digest food and make vitamins
helpful gut bacteria do useful jobs rather than only making us ill
healthy bodies naturally contain helpful gut bacteria
misconceptions: all bacteria are harmful and cause disease
bacteria only cause illness

question: Give three ways that helpful gut bacteria are useful to us.
answer: they help digest fibre
they produce vitamins B and K
they compete with harmful bacteria to reduce their numbers
accept: digest carbohydrates such as fibre
make vitamin B and vitamin K
stop harmful bacteria making us ill
misconceptions: they release toxins to help us
they absorb water for the body

question: How do helpful gut bacteria reduce the number of harmful bacteria in the gut?
answer: they compete with the harmful bacteria
which reduces their numbers and stops them making us ill
accept: they outcompete harmful bacteria for space/food
misconceptions: they release acid to kill them
they eat the harmful bacteria

question: Why are harmful gut bacteria described as harmful?
answer: they release toxins
which can harm us / make us ill
accept: they produce poisons that damage the body
misconceptions: they digest our fibre too quickly
they take up all the vitamins

question: Why is fibre important in our diet, and what does it have to do with gut bacteria?
answer: fibre bulks up food so it passes easily through the digestive system
and fibre is digested by our helpful gut bacteria
accept: fibre helps food move through the gut
gut bacteria break down fibre
misconceptions: fibre is absorbed into the blood for energy
fibre kills harmful bacteria

question: How can a person help keep their gut bacteria working well?
answer: eat a healthy, balanced diet with lots of fruits and vegetables
and avoid things that harm gut bacteria such as a poor diet or certain medications/drugs
accept: eat plenty of fibre, fruit and vegetables
avoid a poor diet that damages gut bacteria
misconceptions: take antibiotics regularly to boost gut bacteria
gut bacteria are not affected by diet

question: A sample of gut bacteria contains 200 bacteria in total. If 150 of them are helpful, how many are harmful?
answer: 50 bacteria
misconceptions: adding to give 350
writing 150 (the helpful bacteria)

question: Sometimes harmful bacteria survive the stomach. Explain how they get past it and what then keeps them in check.
answer: some harmful bacteria make it past the hydrochloric acid in the stomach
the helpful gut bacteria then compete with them to reduce their numbers
accept: a few survive the stomach acid
helpful bacteria keep their numbers down by competing
misconceptions: the stomach kills every single bacterium
there is nothing to control harmful bacteria once past the stomach

question: Which two vitamins are produced by helpful gut bacteria?
answer: vitamin B and vitamin K
options: vitamin B and vitamin K
vitamin C and vitamin D
vitamin A and vitamin E
vitamin C and vitamin K
distractors: gut bacteria do not make vitamin C or vitamin D
gut bacteria make B and K, not vitamin A or vitamin E
only vitamin K is correct here; gut bacteria make B not C
misconceptions: gut bacteria produce vitamin C
gut bacteria produce vitamin D

question: What are gut bacteria?
answer: microbes in the large intestine that help us digest food
accept: helpful bacteria living in the gut/large intestine
misconceptions: bacteria that only cause disease
viruses in the intestine

question: Name the two types of gut bacteria and say where both are mainly found.
answer: helpful and harmful bacteria
both are mainly found in the large intestine
accept: good and bad gut bacteria
they live in the large intestine
misconceptions: they are all helpful
they are mainly found in the stomach

question: A person takes a course of medication that kills many of their gut bacteria. Suggest two problems this could cause for their digestion and health.
answer: less fibre is digested by the gut bacteria
fewer vitamins B and K are produced / harmful bacteria are less well controlled so the person may become ill
accept: they may struggle to digest fibre
harmful bacteria can grow more easily and make them ill
misconceptions: it would have no effect because all bacteria are harmful
killing gut bacteria always makes you healthier
